summary: The source outlines Martha Nussbaum's characterization of the general capability approach. It highlights her view that the approach consists of two clusters of work (quality of life and justice) united by a focus on what people are able to do and be, alongside five core principles including treating individuals as ends and focusing on freedom over mere achievements.

principles.statement: Treat each person as an end.
principles.rationale: This is a fundamental commitment of the capability approach, ensuring that individuals are valued for their own sake rather than as means to other ends.
principles.source_quote: treating each person as an end

principles.statement: Focus on choice and freedom rather than achievements.
principles.rationale: Freedom to achieve well-being is defined by what people are effectively able to do and be, prioritizing their agency and opportunities over forced or accidental outcomes.
principles.source_quote: a focus on choice and freedom rather than achievements

principles.statement: Government has an urgent task to address entrenched social injustices.
principles.rationale: The capability approach is deeply concerned with systemic inequalities and assigns the state a critical, active role in resolving them.
principles.source_quote: being deeply concerned with entrenched social injustices; and ascribing an urgent task to government

principles.rationale: A core commitment of the capability approach is recognizing that there are multiple, distinct, and irreducible human values.

frameworks.name: The General Capability Approach
frameworks.when_to_apply: When evaluating comparative quality of life or theorizing about justice.
steps: Focus on what people are effectively able to do and be.
Treat each person as an end.
Prioritize choice and freedom over achievements.
Assign an urgent task to government to address these injustices.
frameworks.source_quote: consisting of two clusters of work, one focussing on comparative quality of life and the other on theorising about justice. The two clusters share a focus on what people are able to do and be as well as a commitment to five principles

mental_models.name: Capabilities vs. Achievements
mental_models.description: Evaluating well-being based on the freedoms and opportunities a person has (what they are able to do and be) rather than just the final outcomes they achieve.

anti_patterns.description: Focusing strictly on achievements rather than choice and freedom.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It ignores the importance of human agency and the fundamental freedom to choose the kind of life one is effectively able to lead.
